pathophysiology_summary: Acute diffuse otitis externa is inflammation/infection of the external auditory canal, usually bacterial (Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Staphylococcus aureus), precipitated by canal moisture or trauma disrupting the protective cerumen barrier. Oedema and desquamated debris obstruct the canal producing conductive hearing loss and otorrhoea; the richly innervated canal skin produces severe pain with tragal pressure and pinna traction and on jaw movement (shared TMJ/canal innervation). The condition is benign and self-limiting with topical therapy, but the key ground-truth task is to distinguish it from necrotising (malignant) otitis externa — an osteomyelitis of the skull base seen in diabetics/immunocompromised, and to recognise that ototopical aminoglycosides/quinolone-steroid drops require an intact tympanic membrane, which cannot be confirmed remotely.

differential.evidence_basis: Unilateral otalgia + purulent otorrhoea + blocked/muffled ear + pain on external ear contact strongly suggests canal pathology.
Also causes otalgia and conductive loss, but pain is not typically reproduced by pinna traction and usually follows a URTI prodrome; discharge implies TM perforation. Needs otoscopy to distinguish.
Must be actively excluded in any severe OE — screen for diabetes/immunosuppression, granulation tissue, peri-auricular/mastoid spread, cranial neuropathy.

differential_progression.scoring_note: OE may lead, but AOM and necrotising OE must both appear; remote confidence is limited without otoscopy.

differential.evidence_basis: No diabetes/immunosuppression, no mastoid spread, no facial weakness, no high fever — necrotising OE features absent. Uncomplicated AOE remains most likely.
Red-flag screen negative — but remains the escalation target if any red flag emerges.
Cannot be fully excluded without otoscopy; management overlaps in requiring in-person examination.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative red-flag screen, uncomplicated AOE leads; the correct action is same-day in-person otoscopy + topical therapy, not remote prescription.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth; requires otoscopy to confirm, exclude TM perforation before ototopical drops, and exclude AOM/foreign body.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth position is presumptive AOE requiring same-day in-person examination.

red_flags.description: Diabetes mellitus or immunocompromise in a patient with severe OE
red_flags.clinical_significance: Major risk factor for necrotising otitis externa (skull-base osteomyelitis); its presence would mandate urgent ENT/ED assessment and imaging.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Necrotising OE progresses to cranial neuropathies and intracranial spread with high morbidity/mortality.

red_flags.description: Peri-auricular/mastoid swelling or erythema, facial nerve weakness, or high fever/systemic toxicity
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates spread beyond the canal (mastoiditis, necrotising OE, facial nerve involvement) requiring emergency assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed recognition of skull-base or intracranial complication.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Prescribing ototopical quinolone/aminoglycoside drops over chat without confirming an intact tympanic membrane — aminoglycoside drops are ototoxic through a perforation. The telehealth-correct action is same-day in-person otoscopy.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Failure to screen for necrotising OE
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on 'simple ear infection' and not asking about diabetes/immunosuppression or peri-auricular spread, missing the important-not-to-miss diagnosis.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Otoscopy of the external canal and tympanic membrane — cannot confirm AOE vs AOM, exclude TM perforation, or see granulation tissue
Pinna traction / tragal tenderness sign — cannot be performed by the clinician remotely
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Same-day in-person review (GP/urgent care) for otoscopy and topical therapy; ED/urgent ENT only if necrotising-OE red flags present.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Prescribing ototopical drops remotely without excluding TM perforation risks ototoxicity; missing necrotising OE in an undisclosed diabetic risks skull-base osteomyelitis.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Topical therapy (e.g. quinolone ± corticosteroid, or acetic acid) is first-line for uncomplicated AOE after otoscopic confirmation and exclusion of TM perforation; aural toilet aids penetration. Consider necrotising OE in diabetic/immunocompromised patients.
